content.text: The Four Golden Signals in an AI Integration Context

Google SRE's four golden signals — Latency, Traffic, Errors, and Saturation — apply directly to Claude integrations, but each requires re-interpretation. A Claude API call is not a typical HTTP request: it can stream tokens for 30 seconds, it consumes a token budget rather than network bandwidth, and its errors include AI-specific states like rate limiting and context-length exceeded.
This sub-subtopic maps each golden signal to the specific metrics, PromQL queries, and alert thresholds appropriate for a Claude-powered service.

attrs.alt: Four golden signals mapped to Claude-specific metrics and recommended alert thresholds

content.text: Latency

Latency in a Claude integration has three distinct components that must be measured separately: the time to receive the first token (TTFT), the time to complete the full response, and the end-to-end time including retry overhead.
Latency Metric
PromQL Expression
SLO Relevance
p50 end-to-end
histogram_quantile(0.50, rate(claude_api_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m]))
Typical user experience
p99 end-to-end
histogram_quantile(0.99, rate(claude_api_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m]))
SLO boundary — must stay below target
Time-to-first-token (streaming)
histogram_quantile(0.95, rate(claude_stream_first_token_seconds_bucket[5m]))
User perception of responsiveness
Distinguishing Slow Success from Slow Failure
A 35-second request that times out and a 35-second request that succeeds look identical in a histogram. Segment latency by status label:

# p99 latency for successful requests only
histogram_quantile(0.99,
  sum(rate(claude_api_request_duration_seconds_bucket{status="success"}[5m])) by (le, model)
)

# p99 latency for timed-out requests (should be near your timeout value)
histogram_quantile(0.99,
  sum(rate(claude_api_request_duration_seconds_bucket{status="timeout"}[5m])) by (le, model)
)

If you have only a single latency histogram without a status label, you cannot tell whether a p99 spike means slow successful responses or an increase in timeouts. Add the status label from the first day of instrumentation.
Traffic
Traffic for a Claude integration is measured in two units: requests per minute (for rate limiting and capacity planning) and tokens per minute (for budget monitoring and cost reporting). Track both.

# Request rate by model (requests per minute)
sum(rate(claude_api_requests_total[1m])) by (model) * 60

# Input token rate by model (tokens per minute)
sum(rate(claude_api_tokens_input_total[1m])) by (model) * 60

# Output token rate (usually more expensive per token than input)
sum(rate(claude_api_tokens_output_total[1m])) by (model) * 60

# Queue depth (leading indicator of traffic exceeding worker capacity)
claude_worker_queue_depth
Traffic Anomaly Detection

A sudden 20% traffic drop often indicates an upstream failure (callers cannot reach your service) rather than a slow-down. A sudden 3× spike may indicate a runaway batch job or a misconfigured retry loop.

# Detect traffic drop > 20% vs 30-minute average
(
  sum(rate(claude_api_requests_total[5m]))
  /
  sum(rate(claude_api_requests_total[30m]))
) < 0.80

# Detect traffic spike > 3× vs 30-minute average
(
  sum(rate(claude_api_requests_total[5m]))
  /
  sum(rate(claude_api_requests_total[30m]))
) > 3.0
Errors

Claude API errors fall into four categories, each with a different operational response. Measuring only total error rate misses the signal — the category determines whether you page on-call immediately, wait to see if retries resolve it, or investigate a bug.
Error Category
HTTP / Exception
Operational Response
Rate limit
429 / RateLimitError
Monitor for sustained 429s (quota exhaustion vs burst). Burst: retries resolve. Quota: request increase.
Timeout
socket / APITimeoutError
Check if p99 latency correlates. Check Anthropic status page. May be systemic or your own timeout too short.
Server error
500, 529 / InternalServerError
Check Anthropic status page. Retry-eligible; if persistent, escalate to Anthropic support.
Client error
400 / BadRequestError
Bug in your application — wrong message format, prompt too long, invalid parameter. Never retry.
Auth error
401 / AuthenticationError
API key problem — expired, wrong environment, rotation issue. Page immediately.

# Overall error rate (all types)
sum(rate(claude_api_errors_total[5m])) by (service)
/
sum(rate(claude_api_requests_total[5m])) by (service)

# Rate limit error rate specifically (429s)
sum(rate(claude_api_errors_total{error_type="rate_limit"}[5m]))
/
sum(rate(claude_api_requests_total[5m]))

# Server error rate (500/529) — check Anthropic status if this spikes
sum(rate(claude_api_errors_total{error_type="server_error"}[5m]))
/
sum(rate(claude_api_requests_total[5m]))

# Auth errors — always page; should normally be zero
sum(rate(claude_api_errors_total{error_type="auth_error"}[1m]))
Saturation

Saturation measures how close a resource is to its capacity limit. For Claude integrations, the relevant saturation resources are token quotas, worker concurrency, and queue depth — not CPU or memory.
Token Quota Saturation

Anthropic rate limits are expressed in requests per minute, tokens per minute, and tokens per day. A service approaching its token quota will see increasing 429 errors; measuring quota utilisation directly lets you act before the 429s start.

import anthropic
from prometheus_client import Gauge

quota_used_pct = Gauge(
    "claude_token_quota_used_pct",
    "Fraction of hourly token quota consumed (0–1)",
    ["model", "limit_type"],
)


async def update_quota_gauge(
    client: anthropic.AsyncAnthropic,
    model: str,
    hourly_token_limit: int,
    current_hour_tokens_used: int,
) -> None:
    """
    Call this after each API response to update the quota gauge.
    current_hour_tokens_used must be tracked in your application state.
    """
    quota_used_pct.labels(
        model=model, limit_type="hourly_tokens"
    ).set(current_hour_tokens_used / hourly_token_limit)
Worker Queue Saturation

# Queue fill percentage (0–1); alert when > 0.8
claude_worker_queue_depth / on() group_left() claude_worker_queue_capacity

# Active call saturation: in-flight calls vs configured max concurrency
claude_worker_active_calls / on() group_left() claude_worker_max_concurrency

Saturation metrics are leading indicators. Error rate and latency tell you something is wrong now; saturation tells you something will go wrong soon. Alert on saturation at 80% to give the team time to intervene before the system degrades.
Correlation: Diagnosing With All Four Signals Together
Each golden signal alone answers one question. Together they narrow down the root cause quickly:
Observation
Probable Cause
First Check
Latency ↑, Errors stable, Traffic stable
Model responding slowly; possible API-side slowdown
Anthropic status page; p99 by model to isolate
Errors ↑ (429), Latency stable, Traffic ↑
Token quota being consumed by traffic spike
Token rate chart; check for batch job or runaway caller
Errors ↑ (auth), Latency N/A, Traffic drops to 0
API key revoked or expired
Check Secrets Manager; rotate immediately
Queue ↑, Errors stable, Latency ↑, Traffic stable
Workers too slow; API is fine but processing bottleneck
Worker active_calls vs concurrency limit; check CPU/memory
Traffic ↓ 50%, All other signals normal
Upstream callers cannot reach service
Ingress health; upstream error rate; network partition?
Writing Prometheus Alerts for the Four Signals
Alerts derived from the four golden signals form the minimum alerting set for a Claude integration. The following alert rules use the recording rules defined in earlier sub-subtopics where available.

# prometheus/alerts/claude-golden-signals.yaml
groups:
  - name: claude_golden_signals
    rules:
      # ─── Latency ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
      - alert: ClaudeAPILatencyWarning
        expr: |
          histogram_quantile(0.99,
            sum(rate(claude_api_request_duration_seconds_bucket{status="success"}[5m])) by (le, service)
          ) > 4.0   # 2× a 2-second SLO target
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: warning
        annotations:
          summary: "Claude API p99 latency elevated ({{ $value | humanizeDuration }})"
          description: "Service {{ $labels.service }} p99 success latency is {{ $value | humanizeDuration }}, above 4 s warning threshold."

      - alert: ClaudeAPILatencyPage
        expr: |
          histogram_quantile(0.99,
            sum(rate(claude_api_request_duration_seconds_bucket{status="success"}[5m])) by (le, service)
          ) > 6.0   # 3× a 2-second SLO target
        for: 2m
        labels:
          severity: page
        annotations:
          summary: "Claude API p99 latency critical ({{ $value | humanizeDuration }})"

      # ─── Traffic ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
      - alert: ClaudeTrafficDropWarning
        expr: |
          (sum(rate(claude_api_requests_total[5m])) by (service)
           / sum(rate(claude_api_requests_total[30m])) by (service))
          < 0.80
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: warning
        annotations:
          summary: "Claude API traffic dropped > 20% ({{ $labels.service }})"

      # ─── Errors ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
      - alert: ClaudeAPIErrorRateWarning
        expr: |
          sum(rate(claude_api_errors_total[5m])) by (service)
          / sum(rate(claude_api_requests_total[5m])) by (service)
          > 0.005
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: warning
        annotations:
          summary: "Claude API error rate > 0.5% ({{ $labels.service }})"

      - alert: ClaudeAPIAuthError
        expr: sum(rate(claude_api_errors_total{error_type="auth_error"}[1m])) > 0
        for: 1m
        labels:
          severity: page
        annotations:
          summary: "Claude API authentication error — check API key"

      # ─── Saturation ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
      - alert: ClaudeQueueDepthWarning
        expr: claude_worker_queue_depth / on() group_left() claude_worker_queue_capacity > 0.80
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: warning
        annotations:
          summary: "Claude worker queue depth > 80% capacity"

      - alert: ClaudeCircuitBreakerOpen
        expr: claude_circuit_breaker_state == 2
        for: 1m
        labels:
          severity: page
        annotations:
          summary: "Claude API circuit breaker is OPEN — all calls failing fast"
SLO Error Budget Alerts Derived from Golden Signals
The four golden signals are the inputs to your SLO calculations. Error budget alerts fire when the burn rate — the rate at which error budget is being consumed — is too high.

# Multiwindow, multi-burn-rate alert (Google SRE Book pattern)
# Fires when the 1-hour burn rate is > 14× AND the 5-minute burn rate > 14×.
# This catches both fast burns (spike) and slow burns (sustained degradation).

groups:
  - name: claude_slo_error_budget
    rules:
      - alert: ClaudeHighErrorBudgetBurn
        expr: |
          (
            sum(rate(claude_api_errors_total[1h])) / sum(rate(claude_api_requests_total[1h]))
            > (14 * 0.001)  # 14× the 0.1% error budget threshold
          )
          and
          (
            sum(rate(claude_api_errors_total[5m])) / sum(rate(claude_api_requests_total[5m]))
            > (14 * 0.001)
          )
        for: 2m
        labels:
          severity: page
        annotations:
          summary: "High error budget burn rate — SLO at risk"
          description: "At the current burn rate, the monthly error budget will be exhausted in 2 hours."

      - alert: ClaudeMediumErrorBudgetBurn
        expr: |
          (
            sum(rate(claude_api_errors_total[6h])) / sum(rate(claude_api_requests_total[6h]))
            > (6 * 0.001)
          )
          and
          (
            sum(rate(claude_api_errors_total[30m])) / sum(rate(claude_api_requests_total[30m]))
            > (6 * 0.001)
          )
        for: 15m
        labels:
          severity: warning
        annotations:
          summary: "Elevated error budget burn rate"
          description: "At the current burn rate, the monthly error budget will be exhausted in 5 days."
Dashboard Layout for the Four Signals
A single dashboard that shows all four signals on one screen lets on-call engineers diagnose incidents at a glance. Use the following panel layout:
Row 1 (wide): Service health overview — p99 latency, error rate, request rate, and queue depth as stat panels with colour thresholds (green/yellow/red).
Row 2: Latency — histogram time series showing p50, p95, p99 for each active model. Add a reference line at your SLO target.
Row 3: Traffic — request rate and token rate per minute, segmented by model. Overlay the rate limit ceiling as a horizontal reference line.
Row 4: Errors — stacked bar chart of error_type breakdown (timeout, rate_limit, server_error, auth_error). Fallback activation count as a secondary panel.
Row 5: Saturation — token quota fill gauge, queue depth over time, circuit breaker state (colour: green=closed, amber=half_open, red=open).
Summary

The four golden signals apply to Claude integrations with domain-specific adaptations: latency includes streaming TTFT; traffic is measured in tokens as well as requests; errors are segmented by category because the response differs per type; saturation covers token quotas and worker concurrency, not just CPU.
Measure latency segmented by status label (success vs timeout) so p99 spikes are interpretable.
Track both request rate and token rate; token rate is the budget-relevant signal for rate limiting.
Break errors into four categories: rate_limit (monitor for sustained 429s), timeout (check API status), server_error (retry-eligible), and auth_error (page immediately).
Monitor saturation at 80% threshold for token quota and queue depth to get advance warning before errors start.
Use all four signals together for root cause narrowing: the combination of which signals are elevated and which are stable identifies the failure class quickly.
